turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next Number oh oh nine five seven, Kansas versus Michael Crane. General Stovall. [Inaudible]

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, thank you, and may it please the Court. The Kansas Supreme Court has erroneously read this Court's decision in Kansas V Hendricks as requiring a showing that a potentially sexually violent predator cannot control his behavior and that such a requirement has supplemented the two requisites for civil commitment that this Court has approved in and since the Addington case. Those two requirements, as you know, are mental illness and dangerousness. The volitional impairment that the Kansas Supreme Court has ruled was constitutionally required for civil commitment ignores the fact that psychological disorders, such as antisocial personality, can impair an individual in behavior, cognitive, perceptual, emotional, and even intellectual capacities. This Court has never indicated that there's anything constitutionally significant about a volitional impairment. [Inaudible]

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: There was a good deal of reference in our Hendricks opinion to the to the apparent fact that the person there had what was volitionally impaired. You're saying that was descriptive rather than essential to the holding?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I'm saying it was descriptive. Mr. Hendricks had apparently, and according to himself only, an inability to control his own behavior. And so, when the majority wrote about Mr. Hendricks, they used that kind of a...

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: description. Well, but...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: we also relied on prior authority that made some reference to lack of control.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Well, if this Court says that there needs to be some volitional impairment displayed, we would suggest that it just be merely some impairment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: show.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: think that's the standard adopted in the court below that we're reviewing?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I do.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: Total impairment?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I do.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: do you acknowledge that lack of ability to control one's unlawful conduct and volitional impairment are one and the same thing?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: No, I do not.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Suppose I'm delusional and and I think that that people I see are Satan.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Your Honor, that's...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: You call that a lack of volitional control or d- or delusion?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I would consider that delusional and not a va- a lack of volitional control and the problem with the Kansas Supreme Court's decision is that it says the only thing that we consider is volitional impairment, but there are many kinds of impairments individuals have that are the result of their mental disorder, and so the Kansas court is necessarily limiting the psychiatric diagnosis to say only volitional...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: impairment --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: there I thought there could be cognitive impairments.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Emo-

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: think Justice Scalia has described one.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: could be volitional impairments.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Perceptual, intellectual.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But are these --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: relevant here other than volition?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Which?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: all of those that we mentioned, and perhaps even...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Are relevant in this case?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Not...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Wa-  Yeah, yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: is there any one relevant to the particular individual at issue here other than volition.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I don't believe psychiatrists can tell us what it is that what kind of impairment Mr. Crane has.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: enforcement --

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: the premise that Hendricks proceeded upon.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I'm sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, you're are are you trying to say that what we said in Hendricks was was dictum or...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: was wrong in the in the context of that case?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I'm certainly not saying you were wrong, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, was the Court...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: under some misimpression as as to Hendricks' ability to control himself?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: There wasn't evidence before this Court that would say whether or not Hendricks suffered from a volitional impairment or not.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: That seems to me, that we're back to square one.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Your Honor, I don't believe \noise> it is because Mr. Hendricks, in particular, didn't suffer from a personality disorder.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well,

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: is the test are there different requirements if you proceed based on a personality disorder than if you proceed from a mental abnormality?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Well, the Kansas court seems to think there is because there is no definition in the statute of a personality disorder.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well I was...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: I was suggesting that you thought there was based on the answer you gave to me.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: You think...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: they're both one and the same.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Based on the Kansas Supreme Court decision or based on the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Based...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: on what you think the law ought to be and what the s- s- un- and how the statute is properly interpreted.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: We believe there is no distinction.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: So, anybody with a personality disorder that's a danger to himself or others can be can be civilly committed regardless of volitional control.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: for mental --

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: do you have that you're mentioning, if you look at the definition of personality disorder and they say pick three out of a list of seven, you could pick out habitually doesn't work, doesn't pay debts, is reckless, irritable.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Wha- what what I want to be able to do today, Your Honors, is to convince you that actually that's not true, that an antisocial personality disorder is a severe mental pathology that really does give us sociopaths and psychopaths that cannot conform to our rules.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, your your statute itself, when you're talking about a sexually violent predator, you say mental abnormality or personality disorder, which makes the person likely to engage in repeat acts of sexual violence.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: That's exactly right, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: And I take it what your what your statute is trying to get at is is something more than mere repetitive conduct, mere recidivism.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: And and is the element that distinguishes this kind of behavior from mere repetitiveness some element of lack of control, not necessarily volitional control, but some element of lack of control, perhaps lack of control resulting from delusion, perceptual difficulties and so on, but but some some aspect of the personality that that gives that individual a a a a a lesser chance of controlling behavior in in a way that avoids committing crimes.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: With a slight exception.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: want to be clear that we don't think there needs to be a a third separate, very distinguishable constitutional element...

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: I guess what I'm I guess what I'm getting at is it it seems sensible to call somebody who is just an habitual offender at some dangerous crime or serious crime dangerous.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: That's hundred percent accurate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: predicting who's going to be dangerous...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: That's exactly the issue.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: That they have to have a mental...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Yeah, but what that's the problem in the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: that's not the right definition.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: What is in the statute, a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: severe mental --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: is the definition of the word, mental disorder, that appears in the statute? If a person were to say every person who commits a crime, fifteen times running, is a sociopath and thereby falls within DSM four, you're not going to permit that.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I believe they are there now, that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: All that's there now...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: is mental disorder.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Well, actually it's mental abnormality and...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: personality disorder.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: You and this Court in the Hendricks decision said that pedophilia certainly qualifies...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: as a paraphilia...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: have here we have we have here an antisocial personality disorder.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: And the State's own expert said in Mr. Crane's trial that approximately seventy-five percent of the prison population has antisocial personality disorder.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: And the mental...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: disorder.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: trying...

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: what okay okay...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: But most of them, seventy-five percent of them, was the testimony, suffer from antisocial personality disorder.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I would suggest to you that there doesn't need to be an additional element.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: oh...

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: suffers from that in the deposition, he wasn't he he certainly didn't quote empirical studies to demonstrate that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: percent of the population.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: what is it about them that we can isolate that shows that they are really serious beyond the mere repetition of their crime? In other words, let me put the question this way.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: We have to show a mental illness.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: And and...

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: and that's a psychiatrically-approved condition that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: yeah but any   DSM --

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Then you're just then you're back to...

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg's question, which which which is very much like Justice O'Connor's.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: The actual diagnosis that those folks actually have those diagnosis and are sexually violent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What I would t-

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: then that in the example Justice Ginsburg gave you what was it four out of seven in the list?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: Three of seven.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: That is is as long as as the the the expert witness says, yes, this person i- is subject to four out of those those seven characters they personality traits, that that person, if a sexual offender, could be locked up under the statute?

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: They could be committed for treatment under this this statute, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: And Mr. Crane for what what I would want you to know, though, is that it is not a circu- just because these individuals have committed crimes doesn't mean they have an antisocial personality disorder.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: it it it would be on Justice Ginsburg's example, it would be very easy to prove.

turns.text: It could be if they actually have that diagnosis and have those personality traits and have done that behavior.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: That's the prosecutor then, and but I mean, that's not something that that we would generally do.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Justice Kennedy brought up in in Hendricks a concern, and this case seems to fit that.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: There is a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: reasonable beyond a reasonable doubt, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But it is a civil proceeding.

turns.text: It is, but we have that higher standard.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: May I...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Still, you could you could get to where you were or even beyond.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, what do the annual...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST

turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: it...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And isn't it...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: a frequent case, though, that the psychiatrists say, well, we can't tell until we clinically re-observe him and we can't clinically observe him until he's in a normal environment?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I mean, can psychiatrists --

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: happened in the six so far that that have have been released.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The American Psychiatric Association says in their brief that the, quote, antisocial personality disorder, end quote, which is DSM four at seven oh one seven oh six, there applies to forty to sixty percent among the male-sentenced population.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: In terms of the antisocial personality disorder alone, I don't know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: percent...

turns.text: Alright, see...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER

turns.text: Did they say that forty to sixty percent are beyond a reasonable doubt suffering from a an antisocial personality disorder?

turns.text: I doubt that,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: and I doubt that -- that it applies to --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: is DSM four the standard and if DSM four is not the standard, what is? That's what I think all of us, or several of us anyway, are trying to get to.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: The DSM absolutely is the standard in the psychiatric profession, but it is not the Bible and is not the only thing psychiatrists use.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: May I ask you this question? And I'm and I'm concerned about whether the instructions were adequate and whether you think the instructions were adequate.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: The last, that it's irrelevant to a diagnosis.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: there's there's no need to show any volitional impairment in order a to to obtain a commitment under this statute, so the instruction is correct.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: absolutely the State's position.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: In order to get a diag- in terms of the instructions, that's true because to get a diagnosis, you have to have an impairment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: impairment. So, it's part and parcel.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: in controlling, not not utter inability to control conduct, but difficulty in controlling conduct.

turns.text: she didn't...

turns.text: I I...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: thought I thought you conceded that you have to show difficulty in controlling conduct, and if you don't, this is a quite different case from what I thought.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: w- Well, no -- my predisposes...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: you to do things you want to do.

turns.text: Our --

turns.text: but you say emotional is, and and and I guess em- emotional impairment I suppose would describe e- every sociopath in the country.

turns.text: That is absolutely...

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.text: Kansas...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: court would say that --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: we I think we get back to the point that on your theory any sociopath who has committed a a sexual offense can be committed under this statute upon release.

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: there --

turns.text: It takes three, actually...

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.text: Beyond a reasonable doubt.

turns.text: Again, Your Honor, you're exactly right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: CARLA J. STOVALL
turns.text: It's it's because they're they have a mental abnormality.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, General Stovall.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court, I think the major disagreement between the State State's view of this and Mr. Crane's view is not how dangerous is an individual, but why are they dangerous.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: You have a nice speaking voice, but could you raise it just a little bit? I...

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I'm sorry, Judge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Mr. Crane sought a jury instruction at his trial that was consistent with this Court's decision in Hendricks.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: behavior.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: You say it's defined.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your your Honor, in the Kansas statute itself.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Can you tell us where we find that in the in the papers?

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: It's the first page of the appendix to the petitioner's brief I think.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, joi- joint appendix excuse me.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: We're talking about the instruct- talking about the statute.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: So that a recidivist who will be a recidivist because he's delusional and he thinks that every woman he meets is inviting crude sexual behavior he's fully able to control himself if he doesn't think that the woman is inviting crude sexual behavior, but he happens to think that every woman he meets is inviting him, and he would not be covered because that is not a volitional impairment.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I agree with that, and and may I may I follow that up with perhaps the Kansas Sexual Predator Act has a number of subsections, one of which is directly directly focused on the type of individual you just mentioned in your hypothetical.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Which one -- which one is that? Subsection.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, in the definition of a th- I'm sorry.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, I have it here with a number that I -- you referred to one subsection.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, it's in the statute.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: But you're- you're telling us now what the Kansas Sexual Predator Act does.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, I'm sorry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: at my fingertips.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: here was not that the act didn't cover your client, but you're you're supporting the holding of the Kansas Supreme Court that the act does cover your client, but inasmuch as insofar as it does, it's unconstitutional if it goes beyond volitional impairment.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, th- this the facts of the Crane case dealt specifically with Mr. Crane and Mr. Crane alone .

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Does not abled --

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Ca- ca- can- can I just -- I really don't know what we have before us here.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: That's what the Kansas Supreme Court said.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, if if I have to fault the opinion in the Kan- of the Kansas Supreme Court is it is that it expanded its decision that was directly for Mr. Crane under the specific subsection of the Kansas Sexual Predator Act that dealt with individuals who had been found criminally responsible.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: and that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: you- you say we- we- we found that.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, as as I read Hendricks stands for the proposition that the Kansas act is constitutional because, as with Mr. Hendricks, what it determined was that the State was not seeking to involuntarily commit people based on dangerousness alone, which would have been absolutely unconstitutional under Foucha versus Louisiana.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Which th- which limiting factor was difficulty or impossibility of controlling behavior .

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: The th- I think the exact language of the Kansas statute, or at least of Mr. Hendricks I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I just read th- I just read the portion of the of the opinion that I think the most relevent in what it says is difficult, if not impossible, to control behavior.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: They they seemed to say that if there's no volitional impairment, there cannot be this difficulty or impossibility of controlling behavior.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Well, when Mr. Hendricks professed that he could not control his behavior, that's an indication that when confronted with temptation, he was unable to exercise his free will.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, I'm not trying to limit the what a psychiatrist or a psychologist might be able to say affects abili- the ability of an individual to conform his behavior to society's requirements.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: value.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: would have thought, really, that that is not what we limited it to in Hendricks, that a delusional lack of control would be entirely sufficient constitutionally as a ha- as it relates to a lack of control, that it could be volitional or delusional, that the Kansas court went too far in requiring only volitional as a constitutional standard.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, I would agree with that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes, so if you --

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I I- I agree with that and I hope I haven't misled the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Mr. --

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: that the Kansas Supreme Court went too far.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I agree that they perhaps imposed too strict a limits on these additional elements that have to be found in order to involuntarily commit...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But that there has to be some additional elements.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Absolutely. I sa-

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: the most appropriate one at hand in this case was volitional.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, th- to the first part of your question, the only available argument that we could have made, the only conceivable jury instruction that would have been consistent with the contradictory evidence at trial and this Court's opinion in Hendricks was a a demand for a jury instruction, requiring the jury to find that it was his mental abnormality or his personality disorder that made him be likely to reoffend because it interfered with his ability to control his behavior.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So, what are the words that you want there?

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: That is, imagine I'm talking about the set of people who are very dangerous.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, I I know the State has touted the the descriptive adjective adequate control.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I- I don't I really don't understand where we are now.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, as as I read the the Kansas statute, the legislative body intended that the mental defect cause the individual to be likely to commit future predatory acts of violence.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: It does.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Yes, and this Court...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: And the Kansas Supreme Court didn't think it was okay.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I- I disagree with that.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Well, they have to prove the causality.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: We did object to the jury instruction because we felt it did not adequately address the theme that the State carried to the jury .

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, d- just- just a minute, Mr. Don- The question presented by the State is is in- in its petition for certiorari is- is a very general one, whether the Fourteenth Amendment requires the State to prove that a sa- and I think if you're going to bring up a jury instruction, you're required to cross petition for certiorari and raise that yourself.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Yes, sir, I did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: an objection to the --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: held the jury instruction bad, did it not?

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I'm s- I'm sorry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: ar- on the petition?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: just answer Justice Stevens' question.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I'm sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Can you --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: I- is it not correct that the Kansas Supreme Court held that the jury was not properly instructed?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Was it not properly instructed i- not because it was not instructed in accordance with the Kansas statute, but because if it had been instructed in accordance with the Kansas statute, that would have been unconstitutional.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: What I said, Your Honor, is that the statute requires that an individual susceptible to being involuntarily committed must have a mental illness that makes him or her likely to...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: re-offend.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: what it says, right?

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: The Kansas Supreme Court appeared to hold that a person must be completely unable to control his behavior in order to meet what it thought the constitutional standard is under the due process clause.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I agree with that.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: Hm?

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: It-

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I don't think I do.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Your Honor, I suppose the difference may lie in- in- in what is meant by total or absolute lack of control.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: You can interpret it as you wish.  Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: my interpretation of that perinc- prin- --

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: some of these terms are- are pretty slippery, and, of course, they're all taken in context of what does- does a does a psychiatrist mean by them.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: by --

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: not psychiatrists or psychologists either.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, what...

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: The Kansas Supreme Court quote the very words from Hendricks that Justice Scalia re- referred to before to make this a finding by linking future dangerousness to a mental abnormamality, [:abnormality] a personality disorder, that makes it difficult, if not impossible, to control his behavior? That's what the Kansas Supreme Court repeated.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: and --

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: an- and by doing that, it in essence, if you will, limited the application of the act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: with different effects.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: would- would your objections and the Kansa- and perhaps the Kansas court's objections have been met if instruction number nine at page one fifty-six of the joint appendix said that mental abnormality means a congenital or acquired condition substantially affecting the emotional or volitional capa-

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: If I were to write the instruction, it would have read it- it is a acquired or congenital condition that affects the emotional or volitional capacity to the degree that the person is unable to exercise self-control.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Just what about a person who thinks other people are mo- are like rocks? You know? I mean, he can control himself.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Well, if he's dangerous...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: because --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: he's dangerous because he's autistic or has no sense whatsoever of what a feeling is.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: I would- I would say that he's an appropriate candidate for an involuntary commitment...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Right. And so, what standard there do we use?

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: That he would be...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: unable...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: do it with control because control has to do with volition.

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: This would be a person susceptible to commitment because he's unable to care for himself, and therefore poses...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: a danger for --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: perfectly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JOHN C. DONHAM
turns.text: Yes, it is.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Donham.
